findings.reachability: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber and above) can reach wp_ajax_mk_dp_close_dp_help via admin-ajax.php. No wp_ajax_nopriv_ hook, so unauthenticated users are excluded.
findings.justification: The handler verifies a nonce (nc_help_desk) but performs no current_user_can() check before calling update_option('mk_fm_close_fm_help_c', 'done'), allowing any logged-in user to permanently dismiss the admin help-desk notice for all administrators.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: AJAX handler without capability check
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_ajax_ handler registrations. Any such handler that lacks a current_user_can() call allows any logged-in user to perform the action regardless of role. Nonce checks only prevent CSRF; they do not restrict which users can call the handler.

findings.reachability: The file is included only from duplicate_page_settings() which already enforces current_user_can('manage_options'), so the broken guard has no exploitable impact in the current code path. It becomes dangerous if the file is ever included from an unguarded context.
findings.justification: The guard uses && (AND) instead of || (OR): `!defined('ABSPATH') && !current_user_can('manage_options')`. Because ABSPATH is always defined in WordPress, the first operand is always false, making the entire condition false and the exit() dead code. The intended pattern is `!defined('ABSPATH') || !current_user_can(...)` (two separate guards). A future direct inclusion without the caller's gate would expose the settings page to any logged-in user.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Guard: ABSPATH check joined with AND instead of OR
detection_pattern.description: Finds PHP files that use AND (&&) to join the ABSPATH sentinel with a capability check, making the exit dead code whenever ABSPATH is defined (i.e., always in WordPress). The correct pattern is OR (||): exit if ABSPATH is not defined OR user lacks the capability.

findings.reachability: dp_redirect() is only called in admin-settings.php with hardcoded relative URL strings, so attacker-controlled input does not currently reach it. If any future caller passes user-supplied input (e.g., a redirect_to parameter) the vulnerability becomes exploitable by any user who can reach that caller.
findings.justification: The function applies esc_url_raw() to the URL and then embeds it into a JavaScript string literal via wp_add_inline_script: `window.location.href="$web_url";`. esc_url_raw does not encode double-quotes, so a URL containing a literal double-quote (e.g., foo"bar) would break out of the JS string and execute arbitrary JavaScript. The correct fix is to use json_encode() or esc_js() when injecting values into JS string contexts.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: URL value injected into JS string without JS-safe escaping
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_add_inline_script calls where a URL sanitized only with esc_url or esc_url_raw is embedded into a JavaScript string. esc_url* strips dangerous protocols but does not encode double-quotes, so injecting such a value into window.location.href="..."-style JS is a latent XSS. Values in JS contexts must be escaped with json_encode() or esc_js().

findings.reachability: Reachable by any user who can trigger dt_duplicate_post_as_draft (requires edit_post capability on the target post). The post_type value comes from the database via get_post(), so direct injection requires a prior write to the posts table.
findings.justification: $post->post_type is concatenated directly into the redirect URL without sanitization: `admin_url('edit.php'.$returnpage)` where $returnpage = '?post_type='.$post->post_type. esc_url_raw() is applied to the full assembled URL, but post_type could contain characters that construct an unexpected URL if the DB value were attacker-influenced (e.g., via a malicious post type registered by another plugin). The value should be sanitized with sanitize_key() before concatenation.

detection_pattern.section_name: Open Redirect / URL Manipulation: unsanitized post_type in redirect
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_redirect calls where post_type is concatenated into the URL without sanitize_key(). Although the value comes from the DB, it is still best practice to sanitize it to prevent URL manipulation if the value is ever indirectly attacker-controlled.
